dynamic.architecture_summary: Single-process Flask application exposing a REST API for order management. Domain models (Order, OrderStore) live in src/models.py with an in-memory dict-based store, while src/app.py defines all HTTP routes. Tests use pytest with Flask's built-in test client.

key_components.name: Flask App
key_components.responsibility: Defines all REST API routes (health, create, get, list, cancel orders) and request/response handling.

key_components.name: Domain Models
key_components.responsibility: Contains the Order dataclass and OrderStore in-memory persistence layer.

key_components.name: Order Tests
key_components.responsibility: Test suite covering the order API endpoints.

key_components.name: CI Workflow
key_components.responsibility: GitHub Actions workflow that installs dependencies and runs pytest on push/PR.

agent_safe_operations: Adding new model classes or fields in src/models.py
Adding new test files under tests/
Running pytest tests/ -v to verify changes
agent_forbidden_operations: Replacing the in-memory OrderStore with a database without explicit instruction
Removing or renaming existing API endpoints without explicit instruction
potential_pitfalls: The OrderStore is in-memory — all data is lost on process restart; tests should not assume persistence across test functions unless sharing the same client fixture.
src is a namespace package with an empty __init__.py; Flask is invoked with '--app src/app' which requires the working directory to be the repo root.
The cancel endpoint returns 409 if the order is already cancelled — agents must handle idempotency correctly.
There is no requirements-dev.txt checked into the visible file contents but it is referenced in CI; missing or outdated dev dependencies may cause CI failures.
The .agent-ready/ directory contains a full separate Python project (AgentReady toolkit) — agents must not confuse it with the main application source.
